stem:
The stem-and-leaf plot below shows the scores of $9$ students on a maths quiz.

$$\begin{array}{r|l} \text{Stem} & \text{Leaf} \\ \hline 1 & 3 \quad 5 \quad 7 \\ 2 & 2 \quad 2 \quad 6 \quad 9 \\ 3 & 0 \quad 4 \end{array}$$

Key: $1 \mid 3$ means $13$.

Find the mode.
hint: The mode is the value that appears most often. Look for a repeated leaf on the same stem.
explanation: Reading the plot: $13, \ 15, \ 17, \ 22, \ 22, \ 26, \ 29, \ 30, \ 34$. The value $22$ appears twice; all others appear once. Mode $= 22$.

stem:
The stem-and-leaf plot below shows the times (in minutes) taken by $9$ students to complete a puzzle.

$$\begin{array}{r|l} \text{Stem} & \text{Leaf} \\ \hline 1 & 1 \quad 4 \quad 6 \\ 2 & 0 \quad 3 \quad 7 \\ 3 & 2 \quad 5 \quad 8 \end{array}$$

Key: $1 \mid 1$ means $11$.

Find the median.
hint: The data is already in order. With 9 values, the median is the 5th value.
explanation: Reading the plot in order: $11, \ 14, \ 16, \ 20, \ 23, \ 27, \ 32, \ 35, \ 38$. There are $9$ values, so the median is the $5$th value: Median $= 23$.

stem:
The table below shows the distribution of scores out of $10$ for $12$ students, as represented in a dot plot.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Score} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline 5 & 2 \\ 6 & 3 \\ 7 & 4 \\ 8 & 2 \\ 9 & 1 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Find the mode.
hint: The mode is the score with the highest frequency.
explanation: The score $7$ has a frequency of $4$, which is the highest. Mode $= 7$.

stem:
The stem-and-leaf plot below shows the heights (in cm) of $9$ plants.

$$\begin{array}{r|l} \text{Stem} & \text{Leaf} \\ \hline 3 & 2 \quad 5 \quad 8 \\ 4 & 1 \quad 4 \quad 7 \\ 5 & 0 \quad 3 \quad 6 \end{array}$$

Key: $3 \mid 2$ means $32$.

Find the range.
hint: Range = maximum value − minimum value. Read the smallest and largest values from the plot.
explanation: The minimum value is $32$ (first leaf on stem $3$) and the maximum is $56$ (last leaf on stem $5$). Range $= 56 - 32 = 24$.

stem:
The table below summarises a histogram showing the number of hours $40$ students spent studying in one week.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Hours} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline [0, 2) & 4 \\ [2, 4) & 8 \\ [4, 6) & 12 \\ [6, 8) & 10 \\ [8, 10) & 6 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

How many students are represented in total?

The table below summarises a histogram showing the number of hours $40$ students spent studying in one week.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Hours} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline [0, 2) & 4 \\ [2, 4) & 8 \\ [4, 6) & 12 \\ [6, 8) & 10 \\ [8, 10) & 6 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Find the relative frequency of the $[4, 6)$ class. Give your answer as a decimal.
explanation: The frequency for $[4, 6)$ is $12$. Total frequency $= 40$. Relative frequency $= \dfrac{12}{40} = 0.3$.

stem:
The table below summarises a histogram showing the number of hours $40$ students spent studying in one week.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Hours} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline [0, 2) & 4 \\ [2, 4) & 8 \\ [4, 6) & 12 \\ [6, 8) & 10 \\ [8, 10) & 6 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

How many students studied for fewer than $4$ hours?
hint: Add the frequencies for all class intervals that are less than 4.
explanation: Fewer than $4$ hours includes the classes $[0, 2)$ and $[2, 4)$. Total $= 4 + 8 = 12$ students.

stem:
The table below summarises a histogram showing the number of hours $40$ students spent studying in one week.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Hours} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline [0, 2) & 4 \\ [2, 4) & 8 \\ [4, 6) & 12 \\ [6, 8) & 10 \\ [8, 10) & 6 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Which class interval is the modal class?
hint: The modal class is the interval with the highest frequency.
explanation: The frequencies are $4, \ 8, \ 12, \ 10, \ 6$. The highest frequency is $12$, which belongs to the class $[4, 6)$. Modal class $= [4, 6)$.

stem: A teacher records the individual test scores (out of $100$) for $30$ students and wants to display the distribution of scores. Which display is most appropriate?
hint: Think about what type of data this is and how many values there are.
explanation: The data is numerical and there are $30$ individual values. A stem-and-leaf plot groups values by their tens digit, preserves each individual score, and clearly shows the shape of the distribution. A pie chart is for categories; a bar chart suits categorical or discrete data; a line graph is for data over time.

stem: A survey asks $120$ people to name their favourite type of music. The researcher wants to show the proportion choosing each type. Which display is most appropriate?
hint: Think about whether the data is categorical or numerical, and whether the focus is on proportions.
explanation: Favourite music type is categorical data. A pie chart divides a circle into sectors, making it easy to compare proportions at a glance. A histogram and stem-and-leaf plot are for numerical data; a dot plot suits small numerical datasets.

stem: A scientist measures the heights (in cm) of $1500$ seedlings, which range from $5$ cm to $85$ cm. Which display is most appropriate for showing the distribution?
hint: Consider the size of the dataset and the range of values when choosing a display.
explanation: With $1500$ continuous numerical values across a wide range, a histogram groups values into class intervals, making the distribution shape readable. A dot plot would be overcrowded; a stem-and-leaf plot becomes unmanageable with so many values; a pie chart is for categorical data.

stem: A weather station records the daily maximum temperature each day for one year. Which display is most appropriate for showing how temperature changed throughout the year?
hint: Think about what makes it easy to see change and trends over a sequence of time periods.
explanation: When data is collected at regular time intervals and the focus is on change over time, a line graph is most appropriate. Connecting the data points with a line makes trends, seasons and patterns easy to identify. A histogram and stem-and-leaf plot show distribution but not sequence; a bar chart does not emphasise continuity over time.
